title: Influence of Neighborhood Environments Features on Long-Term Changes in Physical Activity Across the Lifespan
keywords: longitudinal structural path models adjusted for baseline and covariates

summary.hook: This poster reports secondary analyses testing whether baseline activity-supportive neighborhood environment features predict long-term changes in objectively measured physical activity across the lifespan.
summary.summary_30s: This poster reports secondary analyses testing whether baseline activity-supportive neighborhood environment features predict long-term changes in objectively measured physical activity across the lifespan. Data came from four cross-sectional age-specific studies (children, adolescents, adults, older adults), with a fifth study re-recruiting participants for long-term follow-up (5.1 to 15 years) in a combined sample of n=1067. Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) was measured by Actigraph accelerometer at baseline and follow-up, and perceived neighborhood environment by NEWS, with longitudinal structural path models predicting follow-up MVPA from baseline environment features. Conclusions indicate MVPA declines over time in all age groups, but higher activity-supportive neighborhood features—across macro and micro scales—are associated with less decline in MVPA; people in high-supportive neighborhoods show less long-term MVPA decline than those in low-supportive neighborhoods.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Examine how baseline macro- and micro-scale activity-supportive neighborhood environment features are associated with long-term changes in MVPA across multiple age groups (children, adolescents, adults, older adults).

Approach: Population: Participants from four age-specific studies across children, adolescents, adults, and older adults; re-recruited for follow-up into a combined sample. Primary outcome: MVPA at follow-up / change in MVPA (decline).
Finding: MVPA declines from baseline to follow-up in all age groups.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Baseline activity-supportive neighborhood environment features (macro and micro) and overall activity supportiveness are associated with less long-term decline in MVPA across all age groups; individuals in high-supportive neighborhoods show less decline compared with those in low-supportive neighborhoods. Implications (poster-grounded): Findings add prospective evidence for built environment–PA relationships to guide policy and practice for non-communicable disease (NCD) prevention and control across the lifespan. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Not stated on poster (introduction notes cross-sectional evidence and motivates more prospective work). Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Not stated on poster
key_numbers: 5 macro and 3 of 4 micro-scale baseline activity-supportive neighborhood features (and overall supportiveness) were associated with less decline in MVPA across all age groups.
Long-term follow-up ranged from 5.1 to 15 years in a combined follow-up sample of 1067.
Data came from four cross-sectional age-specific studies (children, adolescents, adults, older adults), with a fifth study re-recruiting participants for long-term follow-up (5....
Conclusions indicate MVPA declines over time in all age groups, but higher activity-supportive neighborhood features—across macro and micro scales—are associated with less decli...
takeaways: MVPA declines from baseline to follow-up in all age groups.
Baseline activity-supportive neighborhood environment features are associated with less decline in MVPA across all age groups.
Specifically, “5 macro and 3 of 4 micro-scale” baseline activity-supportive features, and overall activity supportiveness, are associated with less MVPA decline.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What specific macro-scale and micro-scale neighborhood features were included, and which ones were the “5” and the “3 of 4” that predicted less decline?

what_to_ask.question: MVPA declined in all groups—what was the typical magnitude of decline (minutes/day or % change) by age group?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How did you handle missing accelerometer data at follow-up, and did missingness differ by neighborhood type?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: Which built-environment changes are most actionable from your results (macro vs micro features) for cities/communities?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
